nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shores and coral reefs; also occurs in estuaries and silty inner reefs at depths of roughly 1–25 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Reef-associated, non-migratory; typically encountered singly or in pairs.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty foods once settled — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and frozen carnivore preparations are commonly used. New arrivals are often shy and deliberate feeders; live foods (live brine, live blackworms) are commonly required to start them eating. Renowned for picking at Aiptasia (glass anemones).

imageCandidates.notes: Luc Viatour, 4304x2860. Tack-sharp full-body profile; vivid copper/orange vertical bands, black dorsal eyespot, elongated tubular snout and eye bar all clearly shown on a vibrant live individual.
JSutton93, 2455x1790. Sharp, colourful aquarium shot with corals/anemone backdrop; whole fish in frame with all diagnostic bands and eyespot visible.
Bernard DUPONT, 4416x2649. Wild in-situ reef shot showing natural habitat; whole fish and markings visible, slightly softer/hazier than the aquarium shots.

summary: The Copperband Butterflyfish is an Indo-Pacific species prized for its copper-banded body and elongated snout, and is commonly kept as a natural control for Aiptasia glass anemones. It is considered an advanced fish, primarily due to feeding challenges when first introduced.
careNotes: Best housed in a mature, well-established reef or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) aquarium — commonly advised at six months or older — with peaceful tankmates and ample live rock providing crevices for grazing. Generally kept singly unless a confirmed mated pair is acquired together; conspecifics and similar butterflyfishes tend to be quarrelsome. Reef compatibility requires caution: the Copperband has been reported to pick at feather dusters, tube worms, clams, ornamental shrimp, and occasionally LPS polyps (large-polyp stony corals), while it frequently targets Aiptasia (glass anemones). Feeding the new arrival is the chief husbandry challenge; many specimens refuse prepared foods initially and require live brine or live blackworms to be coaxed onto frozen mysis and chopped seafood. Reported aquarium lifespan varies, with some individuals reaching only around four years and others living six to ten years under excellent care.
breedingNotes: Has rarely if ever been bred in home aquaria. Forms monogamous pair bonds in the wild; pair-bonded individuals should not be separated.
